title: Theory for the mixed alkali effect in glasses
authors: Justus Leiber, Quinn Emilia Fischer, Sven Lohmann, Philipp Maass
abstract: The mixed alkali or mixed mobile ion effect in glasses manifests itself by strong nonlinear variations of ionic transport properties upon mixing of different types of mobile ions. We develop a theory for this effect based on thermally activated hopping transport in disordered site energy landscapes that consistently incorporates the statistical-mechanical and kinetic aspects of a mobile ion mixture. This includes a consideration of the joint probability density of site energy states, generalized Fermi distributions for mean site occupations, and cross-terms in the current response described by nondiagonal Onsager coefficients. The theory shows that a mixed alkali effect can arise even when the two ion species share identical site energy distributions. It suffices that sites have distinct energies when occupied by ions of different type. Taking into account that a mismatch energy is needed for ions of one type to occupy sites adapted to the other type, the mixed alkali effect becomes stronger. Spatial correlations between site energies are needed for the mobility of the majority ion to decrease stronger than exponential upon replacement by the minority ion. The theory agrees well with kinetic Monte Carlo simulations. Application to mixed alkali phosphate glasses yields good agreement with measured conductivity activation energies.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive theory for the mixed alkali effect in glasses by linking microscopic energy landscapes to macroscopic transport properties. It explains how energy mismatches and spatial correlations drive nonlinear changes in conductivity. The theoretical predictions show excellent agreement with experimental measurements in alkali phosphate glasses.
main_problem: Developing a theoretical framework to explain the mixed alkali effect (MAE) in glasses, specifically the nonlinear variations in ionic transport properties when different types of mobile ions are mixed.
main_result: The theory demonstrates that the MAE arises from mismatch energy and site energy correlations, and can occur even with identical site energy distributions. It successfully predicts the 'vulnerability effect' and matches experimental activation energies in phosphate glasses.
method: A generalized Ambegaokar-Halperin-Langer (AHL) theory combined with kinetic Monte Carlo simulations and the use of the grand canonical ensemble to derive transport coefficients.
model_or_system: A fermionic hopping model on a disordered lattice representing ion-conducting glasses, featuring site-exclusion interactions and a joint probability density function for site energies of different ion species.
key_observables: Ionic conductivity, mobility, activation energies, partial conductivities, and Onsager coefficients.
important_parameters: Mixing ratio (x), mismatch energy (epsilon_mis), spatial correlation strength (kappa), and site energy fluctuations (Delta).
main_assumptions: Neglect of long-range Coulomb interactions, use of a simple cubic lattice, and the assumption of a constant lower barrier for saddle point energies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows jump rates and energy barriers; Figure 2 shows site occupation in the zero-temperature limit; Figure 3 displays joint PDFs of site energies; Figure 4 illustrates the energy landscape; Figure 5 compares KMC and AHL theory; Figure 6 shows the impact of correlations on activation energies; Figure 7 shows fits to experimental data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the MAE problem, defines the Hamiltonian and statistical mechanics of the site-exclusion model, develops the generalized AHL transport theory, introduces spatial and energy correlations, validates the theory via KMC simulations, and concludes with an application to experimental phosphate glass data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Occupation of sites with energies ϵA and ϵB in the zero-temperature limit. The blue, red and white areas mark the pair of site energies (ϵA, ϵB) occupied by A ions, B ions, and vacancies V, respectively. The bullet marks the point (ϵA F , ϵB F) of Fermi energies. When the mixing ratio is changed between zero and one, it moves along a curve. The dashed line gives an example of such a curve for the joint PDF of site energies discussed in Sec. IV (parameters are the same as those in the uppermost, left panel of Fig. 3).

FIG. 3. Grey-scale plots of joint PDFs ψ(ϵA, ϵB) at a fixed mixing ratio x = 0.5 for symmetric parameters with respect to A and B ions. The left column shows PDFs for vanishing mismatch energy ϵmis = 0 and three onsite energy correlations ρ = 0 (no correlations), ρ = 0.9 (strong positive correlations) and ρ = −0.9 (strong negative correlations). The right col- umn shows PDFs for the same ρ-values but mismatch energy ϵmis = 3. The dashed lines in the ϵA, ϵB-planes separate the areas energetically favorable for A ions (left of diagonal) and B ions (right of diagonal).

FIG. 4. One-dimensional sketch of energy landscapes for A (blue line) and B (red line) ions in the constant lower barrier model for a mixing ratio x = 0.2. The parameters are the ones used for fitting the activation energies of mixed rubidium- lithium phosphate glasses Rbx-Li(1−x)-PO3 in Sec. VII C, see Table I.

FIG. 5. Comparison of results from KMC simulations with the generalized AHL theory for the site energy PDF with symmetric parameter setting and parameter values ϵmis/∆0 = 3 and ρ = 0. (a)-(d) Arrhenius plots of Onsager coefficients Lαβ determined from KMC simulations for four mixing ratios x. (e) Isotherms of KMC simulated partial conductivities σKMC A (x), σKMC B (x), and σAHL A (x), σAHL B (x) calculated from the generalized AHL theory for kBT = ∆0/3.4. (f) Simulated activation energies EKMC A (x), EKMC B (x) and calculated EAHL A (x), EAHL B (x).

FIG. 6. Impact of energy landscape features on the variation of activation energies EAHL A (x) and EAHL B (x) of partial conductiv- ities: data for (a) two mismatch energies ϵmis [Eq. (30)], ρ = 0 [no onsite energy correlations, Eq. (31)] and κ = 0 [no spatial correlations between site energies, Eq. (32)], (b) ϵmis = 3, κ = 0, and two onsite energy correlations ρ = −0.9 and ρ = 0.9, and (c) ϵmis = 3, ρ = 0.9, and two strengths κ = 1/3 and κ = 2/3 of spatial correlations between site energies. Lines are guides to the eye.
